text: The video shows the interior view of the abdomen during a ventral hernia repair. The surgeon pulls down the peritoneum, revealing the fascia, the strongest layer of the abdominal wall. The peritoneum is covered with a yellow layer of adipose tissue.

The surgeon removes the peritoneum from the fascia to prepare for mesh placement, revealing the hernia with yellow adipose tissue protruding through the space. The fascia is skeletonized as the tissue is extracted from the hernia site.

The surgeon visually identifies the true edges of the hernia defect, estimating its size to be approximately three centimeters in diameter using a left-handed instrument as a reference. An eleven point four centimeter circle-shaped mesh is selected for repair. The surgeon continues to separate the peritoneum from the fascia beyond the hernia defect to create a pocket large enough for the mesh. Once sufficient fascia is exposed, the hernia defect will be closed with sutures.

The surgeon tightens the sutures under tension to bring the edges of the hernia defect together. Once closed, the circular mesh is positioned flat against the abdominal wall, ensuring adequate overlap to bind securely around the hernia edges.

The surgeon has separated the peritoneum from the fascia, allowing the mesh to bind directly to the fascia for a strong repair. The mesh is sutured to the abdominal wall using absorbable stitches. The surgeon then closes the pocket in the peritoneum to conceal the mesh from the intestines, reducing the risk of scar tissue formation and small bowel obstructions.
